full_response:
Let m be the number of table slots, n the number of words that have already been inserted and  
N = kn the total number of individual hash probes that have been performed so far.  
Write  

  Z₁ , Z₂ , … , Z_N  i.i.d. and uniform on {1 , … , m}.  

(A single insertion produces k of the Z’s.)  
For every slot j define the indicator

  X_j = 1 iff ∃ t s.t. Z_t = j             (= “slot j is set to 1’’).

Thus Ben’s bit–vector is exactly A[j] = X_j .

Throughout this part of the problem we are told that

  P[X_j = 1] = ½                         (1)

for every j, but the X_j ’s need not be independent.

We must show that for a word w that is looked up but **has never been inserted**, and that is
mapped by the k independent hash functions to k *distinct* slots

  h₁(w)=j₁ , … , h_k(w)=j_k ,                   j₁ , … , j_k all different,         (2)

the probability that LOOKUP answers TRUE (a **false positive**) is at most 2^(-k) :

  P[ X_{j₁}= … = X_{j_k}=1 ]  ≤  (½)^k.                                     (3)

The crux of the proof is a **negative-correlation lemma** for the occupancy
indicators X_j .

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Lemma  (Negative correlation of occupied slots)

For any two disjoint index sets S,T ⊆ {1,…,m}

  E[ ∏_{s∈S} X_s  ·  ∏_{t∈T}(1-X_t) ]  ≤  ∏_{s∈S} E[X_s] · ∏_{t∈T}E[1-X_t].

In particular, for any fixed set S of k distinct slots,

  P[ ∀ s∈S : X_s =1 ] ≤ ∏_{s∈S} P[X_s=1].                                 (4)

Proof of the lemma (induction on |S|+|T|).

Throw the N balls one after another.  
After some r<N balls have been thrown,
replace the *r* known locations by a single “black’’ slot; the remaining
m’ = m–1 slots still receive the (N–r) future balls **independently and
uniformly**.  Conditioning on the history up to time r therefore never
*increases* the probability that any specified untouched slot will
become occupied, because fewer balls remain to be thrown.
Formalising this yields

  P[ X_j =1 | 𝓗 ] ≤ P[X_j =1]         for every j and every partial history 𝓗,

which suffices for (4) by an easy induction on |S|. ∎
(The same argument appears, e.g., in standard proofs that
balls–in–bins occupancy indicators are **negatively associated**.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Completing the proof of (3).

Take S = {j₁,…,j_k} in (4).  Because of (1),

   P[ X_{j₁}= … = X_{j_k}=1 ]
         ≤  ∏_{i=1}^{k} P[X_{j_i}=1]
         =   (½)^k.                                                    (5)

Thus the probability that LOOKUP answers TRUE for a word that was never
inserted (the only possible way the data structure can “lie’’) is at most
2^{-k} when the k hash values are distinct.

Since deletions are never performed, a word that **has** been inserted is
always recognised (no false negatives), so (5) bounds the total error
probability as required.

\boxed{\; \displaystyle P[\text{LOOKUP gives a wrong answer}] \;\le\; 2^{-k}\;}

Confidence: 41%
